Quotation: I think I got it. I think the major factors are very similar for all programming languages. So, basically, you calculate risk, you calculate gains, you calculate the effort you have to put into it. The major triggers are safety; how can I say, the cut of external dependencies, I think that it doesn't matter if you are speaking about a Java or a Python program, you don't want to use unnecessary third-party libraries, which can be changed, which can be outdated, which can be not supported. That's a problem for Java, Python, or C++, also because, of course, the safety and the readability or the learnability of the codes are similar factors. I see the factors are the same, the thresholds are different, it's likely in safety is much more an issue in C++ than, let's say, in Java. And it might be that Python is even a higher issue than in C++ because Python is a more dynamic language with more problems coming out from runtime. And, for example, if you can use any new Python feature typings, if you can use typings as a new language feature to decrease the runtime problems to decrease the possibility of runtime box, that you will press it more than in C++ or in Java to introduce such features. So, I see that the major factors might be the same for every language. And the threshold is different. In C++, it might be that the performance is more important than in Java. Java is that slow anyway, it doesn't matter how you improve it, it's not so much gain. Actually, if you have a web application, a database application, you don't mind the performance at all. Not that much. Okay?

Quotation: One of the most important aspects of evolving code, as the language evolves, is the need for migrating. One notorious language for giving developers headaches because of its ways of evolving is Scala. Its philosophy is: whatever becomes deprecated today, tomorrow will be lost. Code becomes unusable from one version to another, and the cost associated with leaving your code behind by two or three versions of the language is very high, both in time and energy spent in updating it. As a developer, it is very important to your day-to-day work to follow along with the language evolution, because it diminishes the cost of migrating an old codebase, which is hard to deal with, and saves you time down the road. Furthermore, it improves performance, since languages evolve in many aspects. Think about Ruby. From version 2.0 to version 3.0, it took an important step in improving performance. I went to a lecture by Koichi Sasada, who rewrote the entire garbage collector. He managed to make the garbage collector work three times faster, even though he said the goal was to make it ten times faster. Anyway, increasing its performance was an important improvement regarding its competitors, other faster and interpreted and compiled languages, like Python, Java, and C#. It is critically important that you are evolving your codebase as languages evolve so that you get new features, performance gains, security bug fixes, and avoid the trauma of migration.
Codes: Avoiding system obsolescence | Improve code performance | Improve code security | Lack of SCR may introduce technical debt
Concepts: Degradation effects | Performance and Efficiency | Preventing System Obsolescence | Reliability and Correctness
SubCategories: Challenges & Impediments | Code Quality Improvements | Drivers for Source Code Rejuvenation

And, as I read them, I try to remember that. If I’m writing a code, I try to include this new feature, such as a move constructor for my class, and, as for the compiler, we use (coverity scan). Quotation: You’ll need to pressure the upstream people so you can change too, you see? In case you’re working like this. So like, there’s the compiler change, and depending on how you use the language, in C++, you can incur your code in .... And then a compiler change can cause an incorrect execution, as you were counting on some behavior that’s not defined, or it can cause a performance loss. I’ve seen it happen in the last company that I worked for; we were working with version 14 and we tried to change into 17. When they started making performance tests, there was an (impact) on the performance. Until I left them, the cause remained unidentified. Normally, this is what happens: when you change compilers, the performance gets better. You see? The compiler people are not dumb, they also follow this way of thinking: if you’re making a change, it has to upgrade the security, performance, or usage. And they are very careful with C++ performance. I don’t know what happened, why the performance got worse, but it was detected that it did. And my guess is that someone was using a behavior anti error software in the code and it got a false performance. And maybe in our specific case it wouldn’t have caused any issues, but with the compiler change, the compiler people corrected the problem and it caused an impact. But I don’t know, we didn’t get to investigate it and I left the company before we solved this problem. But there was also another situation. We were on compiler, if I’m not mistaken, 5 or 4 point something, and we were going to compiler 10 - we were going to skip 5 versions. Quotation: It's an important factor, but not the major drive. Usually, we are working with quite complex systems, and we don't refactor them unless we see a really important advantage from this point of view. Let's see a few examples: in C++, well-known issues if somebody is not carefully programming and accidentally happens that you can make memory leaks or different kinds of memory-related issues, that C++ is very well known about. And at Microsoft review, I can send you the reference, it claims that in the last 15 years, more than 80% of the security patches were related to memory issues. Okay, that's at last 15 years on average. Now, of course, when C++ 11 introduced smart pointers, and other techniques, which we use for the danger of these problems, we happily, and almost immediately started to rewrite the code using these new features. But this is almost exceptional, it's typical if you have a large industry code, which works more or less fine. If you have no if you don’t have something exception or trigger, you don't touch that part. First of all, much more danger comes from rewriting the code and introducing new techniques, which you don't want to take if you have no... if there’s nothing... okay, not nothing, but it's not an exceptional advantage you hope for. If you experience regular memory issues, okay? Which demands lots of time and effort to be fixed, of course. You’re happy, accept these new language features and change them. If you see new language features that solve something more elegantly, you say: “Ok, next time, I will use that, but I won’t touch my code. It's working, I have no time and resources to rewrite it. And I don't want to take the risk of a new box.”.

I'll focus on the use of records, for example. It's not exactly the amount of lines, but it makes the code much more simplified. Let's say you're creating a DTO, which is an object for data transfer.

If you create using a class, you need to implement the constructor, GET, set, equals, toString, and so on. In the record, it eliminates most of these things. You practically declare a public record, the class name, attributes, and it's basically done.

It has a higher speed, it guarantees some things like immutability, so it brings some gains. This issue of being more legible, because there are fewer things in the class, so you blink and you already know what it is. You guarantee some things just by using the record.

If you implement in a class, you can't create the set methods, for example, to ensure that it is immutable. In the record, you don't need that.

Quotation:
W: I see. And what reasons lead you to evolve code into supporting new language features?

R: Data protection, security.

W: I see. Security is the major reason that leads you to evolving code?

R: That’s right, memory leak.

W: Got it. Could you tell me more about it?

R: There used to be many constructions made based on vectors that, at some point, would corrupt the memory. And from that point on, it was a time bomb, that will explode sometime. But then you’d start to upload absurd data, and start getting complaints from above, people complaining that things that shouldn’t happen are happening from a memory leak. Other than that, there’s the concurrency issue, that gets access to everything and you have no guarantee that the data was being protected. Another problem with parallelism was having to postpone .... One piece of data waiting for another, then for another one, causing issues on the run and the application to freeze.

Quotation:
When comparing the release of each feature with the onset of generalized adoption trends, we observe substantial delays for features introduced in Python 3.0. Keyword-only arguments, released in December 2008, reached their adoption onset only in February 2012, corresponding to a delay of approximately 3.2 years. Similarly, the nonlocal statement, also released in December 2008, exhibited adoption onset in October 2012, after about 3.8 years. 

Function annotations, despite being introduced in Python 3.0, entered a generalized growth phase only in October 2016, resulting in a markedly longer delay of nearly 7.8 years. Extended iterable unpacking followed a comparable long-term pattern, with adoption onset observed in September 2019, almost 10.8 years after its release. This slow and staggered adoption aligns with the findings of Di Grazia and Pradel [25], who reported that, despite steady growth since 2017, the overall coverage of function annotations remained low—only about 8% of function arguments and return types were annotated in 2021. 

Together, these results indicate that although several Python 3.0 features are present in a non-trivial fraction of projects (e.g., function annotations appear in nearly 59% of projects in our dataset), their integration into individual codebases unfolds as a gradual rejuvenation process, characterized by incremental and localized adoption over extended periods. 

For Python 3.3, adoption delays are noticeably shorter. The yield from expression, released in September 2012, reached its adoption onset by May 2014, approximately 1.7 years later. Likewise, suppressing exception context, introduced in the same release, entered its growth phase in January 2020, reflecting a longer but still moderate delay of about 7.3 years. Python 3.5 features display heterogeneous adoption dynamics. Coroutines (async/await), released in September 2015, reached generalized adoption by September 2016, after only 1 year. In contrast, additional unpacking generalizations required around 2.6 years, with growth beginning in April 2018. 

Features introduced in Python 3.6 generally exhibit faster adoption. Formatted string literals (f-strings), released in December 2016, reached widespread growth by January 2019 (about 2.1 years), while variable annotations followed closely, with adoption onset in May 2018 (approximately 1.4 years). Finally, among later features, assignment expressions (Python 3.8) show one of the shortest delays: released in October 2019, they reached adoption onset by January 2021, after only 1.3 years.
Documents: 34-SCP - “It Makes the Code Clearer”: Why Developers Adopt Modern Python Features in Open Source Projects
Codes: Delayed adoption of new versions
Concepts: Adoption Strategy and Timing

In this case, we need a detailed plan to evaluate the impacts, how much time would it take to migrate, if we have enough people to execute it, to test it, to put up an introduction. It depends a lot on the company. I have worked for three different companies here in Quebec, and the one I’m working for right now is pretty obsolete in terms of technology, so any migration process, or any new thing needs very detailed planning, containing the changes’ impact. Before this company, I was in another one that had a much better developed method. We worked on an 80/20 scale, being 80% for new features, and 20% for technical debt of the project. We always worked on the languages’ framework updates, performance improvement, error tracking, logs, this kind of thing. So, it depends a lot on the company’s working process.

Quotation: The third thing is, that if you have something with an extraordinary performance gate, performance is still important in certain projects. You know, if you have a Java project, or web application or database application performance, it is not that important, but if you are in investment banking and you are an automatic trading system, then 2% speedup can mean billions of dollars a year. Picture that. Or you just think about the Mars Rover, which was programmed in C++, 2% improvement in efficiency might mean that the solar energy is just enough for another two months. So, C++ is a specific language, which is mostly used when performance issues are coming into the discussion. I see certain situations when there are new C++ solutions, which are just performing better, and that can be an issue, but that is compared to the previous two walls. So, the standard stuff or the safety stuff is less important in many applications, you know, because there are special applications, of course, when this can be important, but in most situations, this is less important compared to other stuff.
